replies.title: Candle Blessing
replies.content:
If I may add something to help the questioner about white candle blessings. I learned the following from one of the various teachers I admire (Sadhguru Jaggi Vasudev).

Find or create your own small and simple vegetable or ghee oil lamp. You can download a $3 book from Amazon on how to make them. Or you may be able to find the info for free online. You could also try using a real (not fake) beeswax candle while blessing but I don't know if you get all of the same benefits as with the vegetable oil lamp. I use the candle but my goal is to make one of these simple vegetable oil lamps and see if I get even better results.


The reason is this: When one lights an oil lamp every one of the Great Elements comes into play - earth - the physical lamp that is the stable base, water - represented by the oil, fire - present by of course the candle flame and air - the element that allows the flame to burn. When all of these come together the final and most subtle of the Great Elements is there too - Aether. Aether creates an atmosphere that is favorable for meditation, blessings and other activities like mantras and yantras.

Sadhguru Jaggi Vasudev says yogis have always known the effect this combination has on an atmosphere around it. He said even Joe Q. Public storytellers at a campfire notice the heightened effect such a combo has on their fellow camping buddies by making them more receptive to the storyteller's tale.

Vinegar is a magical eraser; you can keep noxious energies from manifesting in a room by leaving a little bowl with some vinegar in it on a piece of furniture, and letting it evaporate; in Taoist magic, one effective way to banish noxious energies is to heat an iron poker red hot and plunge it into a bucket of vinegar, causing fumes to boil up and fill the area. Dressing the candle with vinegar would do a fine job of ensuring that anything you did thereafter, until such time as the vapors dispersed, would be magically inert.

I've posted questions here previously regarding my two-year-old's clairvoyance. (I did not, at first, understand what clairvoyance was.) I have another question, but first a recap/update for context.

My daughter sees people's aura as colors. From what I can tell, she perceives people as having one "fixed" color which doesnt change, but the color of a person's face can change with emotion. She rarely talks about this anymore, since people laugh at her when she does.

Despite the silence, I suspect she still sees auras because she is so hungry for color-based vocabulary. She can verbally identify different shades of the same color, whether something is "light" or "dark", and talks about blends like orangey-pink or purplely-blue. She also has invented words for colors that don't seem to exist. (She asked me for a "doodah" colored crayon and was frustrated by my inability to produce one. She has a large crayon collection and none of them suited.)

The rare times she mentions it, rather than say that people "have" or "are" colors, like she used to, she talks about their "favorite" color. This can get amusing, when she insists that someone is wrong about which color is their "favorite."

A recent development, one that is making me re-examine my biases and responses, is that my daughter told me she saw me driving, even though I have never driven in her presence. I recently got around to getting a learner's permit and am taking lessons.

I assumed that she mis-spoke. That she had "heard" about me driving. But she was adamant that she SAW me driving, and it was clearly a big deal to her. It wasnt until she got distressed by my repeated attempts to correct her (why was I so invested in correcting her, anyway?) that it even occured to me that maybe she did see me. That possibility immediately scared me (in a scared-FOR her, not scared-OF her, way). I set that aside and apologized and told her that I believed she saw me. She was content with that and hasnt brought it up since. My visceral fearful reaction, and my overall relationship to magic, is clearly something I'm going to have to process. I assume that some combination of journaling, meditation, and prayer is warranted here.

And finally, the issue for which I have questions for you.

Something frightened my daughter at the playground this past Friday. We were there with a group of my mom friends and their children of varying ages, and I could see my girl safely sitting on a bench with a high vantage point on the playground equipment (her "castle"). I was talking to someone else, and when I looked back to her she was running down the little steps and towards me. She said repeatedly that she was scared, that "it" scared her, and she clung to me and wanted me to hold her. I kept asking what scared her and she at first couldn't seem to find an answer. Finally she said something that sounded like "the foof-TA-woo". I could get no other answer or detail except that the fooftawoo scared her. It sounds like one of her invented words, rather than bad enunciation. She sat on my lap, nursed (something I usually dont let her do in public since breastfeeding a two year old is socially a bit weird) and then she eventually ran off to play without further incident.

Earlier today, she was in her car seat next to me, in a car driven by a Lyft driver, when she started "blowing raspberries" intently for a few minutes without stopping. This is not typical behavior for her at all. Finally I asked her what she was doing. She paused long enough to tell me "I'm blowing the fooftawoo away" and resumed with her noisy raspberries. She did not look or sound scared. When she was finally done she seemed pleased with herself, so I'm assuming whatever she was trying to do was successful. Come to think of it, it was similar to the way she reacts if gets bath water in her mouth, except that she expels the water in seconds, and she was working on expelling the fooftawoo for at least 4 minutes, maybe more.

Any idea what this is about? Is encounters along these lines a common experience for toddlers? Any thoughts as to why blowing raspberries would help in this case?

More importantly: Is there anything I should be doing to prevent things like the fooftawoo, or worse, from bothering her?

I admit that it feels very silly for me to ask this- to treat it like it's something real. But I dont know that it's not, and it feels mych more foolish to dismiss it without trying to understand.

Prayer seems like a preventative approach (at least, it's a very comfortable one for me. I should have done it sooner, but I forgot about fooftawoo until she brought it up today). The bath mentioned in your FAQ- would this be a potential application for it, if she does encounter something she doesnt like again? If so, does it work if she will not stand for the entirety of the bath? And when I'm done and drain the water, reserving some, is it okay for me to immediately refill the tub with water and let her play before going out to dump the container? She has never had a bath without playtime being part of it, so it would be difficult to extract her quickly.

I've also read you recommending setting vinegar out to evaporate. I have a diffuser I use with essential oils. Would diffusing vinegar (which I'd need to do for a bit to clean it every so often) serve the same function?

Thank you for this space to ask questions, especially as I have no one else I could talk to about this.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Maurice Sendak ("Where the Wild Things Are") once wrote this about his own childhood:

People say, "Oh Mr. Sendak. I wish I was in touch with my childhood self, like you." As of it were all quaint and succulent. like Peter Pan. Childhood is cannibals and psychotics vomiting in your mouth. ..... In reality, childhood is deep and rich. It's vital, mysterious and profound. I remember my own childhood vividly ... I knew terrible things ... but I knew I mustn't let adults know I knew. It would scare them.


To judge by my own vividly remembered childhood, Sendak is entirely right about this. Like him, I learned quite early that I must never tell adults about all the terrible things that I could perceive and know without even trying, especially when the things I perceived and knew were true things about those same adults that they could barely bring themselves to face even in their own most private inner selves, or even things that adults had no way of perceiving and sensing themselves. The world your daughter is growing up in is full of quite real things that other children never have to deal with, and that most adults think are just childish fantasies that should be repressed and discouraged.

As a child, most other children seemed to me to be stolid creatures, all activity without any deep seeing or knowing. Only a few--mostly girls--seemed to sense and know the sort of things I could sense and know. But we didn't talk openly about them, even among ourselves when no adults were around. (Now, in my old age, I think that was due to a healthy inborn sense of self-preservation.)

Your daughter seems to me to be the same sort of child I was more than 70 years ago. Perhaps, therefore, she may resemble me in another respect, too: she may have inborn defenses, and even a sort of internal "teacher" who shows her how to defend herself against the things that the adults in her family can't perceive at all, and therefore are helpless to protect her from. I suspect that's how she got the idea of blowing the fooftawoo away, since it seemed to work. I regard that as a very good sign indeed!

(Not all--not even most--of these things are dangerous, though some of them are strange enough to scare a child. Probably her fooftawoo, whatever it was, was just one of those strange things. Most of them just are there, and take no heed of people, even of a child who can actually perceive them.)

So I would say, Don't fear for her. She'll almost certainly grow up safely and become a grown-up you can be proud of, just as I did. But she'll have to do that far more on her own than most children ever have to. You and her father can help her do that, of course, mostly by just standing by in case of trouble from freaked-out adults. You will indeed need to protect her from other adults whom she accidentally terrifies while she is still feeling her way to her own full adulthood.

replies.content:
You are very welcome!

As for trouble from freaked-out adults, yes, I did mean to imply more than merely ridicule or ostracism. But I only hinted, because I didn't know how ready you might be to hear more.

If adults are scared enough, they can easily resort to violence against a child, sometimes even physical violence (rarely even lethal violence).

More likely, though, adults' violence against "scary" children will take other forms than physical: they will resort to societal or psychological (or even medical) violence instead. I think that the greatest dangers for your daughter will likely come from a few--far from all, fortunately--of the adults who work in the institutions that are supposed to facilitate the smooth integration of a child into adult society: school administrators (plus a few teachers) and social workers.

It is so easy for school administrators to track "different" children into the classrooms which serve as the dumping grounds for "difficult" children, which basically are the first step in the school-to-prison pipelines that are so profitable for some of the wealthiest elite. My parents had to defend me from that danger at various points in my childhood. They had an easier time of it than some parents might have, simply because I had a stellar academic record and had impressed many of my teachers. But even so, they had to fight for me at times. A generation later, we had to fight the school system for our children, who were "different" from the norm in other ways than I was. (I had no doubt that I could win that fight in the end, but it was a very real fight nonetheless.)

As for social workers, some of them--again, far from all--are stupid enough to think that "different" children are just societal problems for which either the parents' life-style or the child's biology is to be blamed, and which must be solved by any means available, however brutal. If all you have is a hammer, everything looks like a nail; and the handiest hammers that budgetary constraints leave for social workers are either forced medication or forced removal from the birth family. White middle-class privilege, if you can deploy it, has its definite uses in defending your child from these dangers. Political connections are almost as useful, if you have already made them within the power-structures of your community. If you have such things, work them for all you're worth!

Best wishes for overcoming the challenges you and your daughter will be facing.

replies.title: Daughters clairvoyance
replies.content:
Hi

I would like to say thank you for being open to learning about your daughters clairvoyance. For her the things she sees are just as real as a big angry dog is to you, and just as scary!. I have seen several people posting on reddits paranormal or occult threads asking questions along the lines of "am i nuts", "why do i keep seeing these things" while under a great deal of stress because what they're experiencing is "impossible". Far better to understand it from a young age.

May I ask what faith you follow, if you are ok with sharing that. I find that a two way conversation with a god can help immensely when you have no idea what to do.

Encourage her to talk to you in private about what she sees. Let her know it's real but not many people can see it and almost nobody believes it. Maybe encourage her to link emotions to the colours she sees so she can use it to understand the people she meets. I can imagine this would be very useful as she grows up.

I would encourage you to learn about the things beyond the physical that your daughter is seeing, I can suggest some books that have helped me understand the non physical world. If you have a little background then you can help her more.

Best wishes and blessings on you and your daughter.

replies.title: Re: Daughters clairvoyance
replies.content:
Thank you for your kind response.
After my reaction to my daughter "seeing" me drive, it occured to me what an isolating experience clairvoyance could be. I was quick to discount her claim, insistent on "correcting" her, very slow to consider that the experience was real, and reacted with fear when I did. I'm overall embarrassed by how I behaved, but she seems not to hold it against me and hasnt stopped talking to me about her experiences. Even if she has to navigate this largely on her own, I hope she continues to feel that she can talk to me when she wants.
I dont mind sharing; I am an evangelical Christian. I wasnt bringing it up mainly because assumptions made about my beliefs and practice arent always accurate, and it's generally easier not to get into it unless asked. Also, half my family are Hindu, the other half are a hodge podge of secular/agnostics, Christians of varying denominations, a few who practice Santeria, and at least one who says she "takes the best of all religions and makes her own." So I figured early on that faith isn't a subject to be brought up unsolicited.
I am open to learning, and please feel free to let me know what resources you found useful.

replies.title: Re: Daughters clairvoyance
replies.content:

The wold we used to live in, remembering the powers of the medicine men
How to develop clairvoyance by W E Butler
How to read the Aura by W E Butler
the etheric astral mental and causal bodies of man (heavy going but very informative although slightly old fashioned in some parts)

I have all these in PDF's and could send them to you if you like

By reading about it and getting to know what she may be experiencing or at least where it comes from you can remove your own fear of the unknown and respond to her more naturally.

replies.title: (no subject)

Magic defenses against what? I know of spells to protect houses from fires, from vermin, from sickness, from fae, from unwanted visitors, from law enforcement, from poverty, from domestic strife, from vampires ... but those are all separate spells and one does not take the place of another. replies.title: Re: yasukuni shrine
replies.content:
I'm not at all sure the Japanese approach is the best one, but it certainly beats the stuffing out of the way that Germany handled it. If you were to march around with a placard today that said "Trump is Tojo!" the vast majority of people wouldn't have a clue who you were talking about. Instead, by turning Hitler into the collective Shadow (in Jung's sense) of an entire nation and culture, he's been given immense power -- in a very real sense, he's stronger now than he was when he was Chancellor.

Consider this. You can draw a little black square for the mustache, a diagonal slash for the hairline, nothing more, and practically everyone in the world will instantly know who you've just represented. Can you think of any other human being anywhere for whom you can do something like that and get the same result?

I think it's too late for a German Yasukuni shrine to have any real effect, though. Hitler has become a strange attractor in the collective psyche of the Western world, and I suspect his phantasmal image is going to be having an effect for a very long time to come.

As for Hitler's soul, I suspect he's had ten or twelve short brutal lives already, each of which has ended by being kicked to death by thugs or something of that sort. He's going to have a lot to work through, not least because he had the basic occult background to know better.

replies.title: drowsiness as rebellion of the mind
replies.content:
I've done a related exercise that the mind rebels against that might be instructive.

It is a focus exercise where one keeps 4 pieces of information in ones memory at a time. For me it was colors with a partner. So I had tiles or cards with one color on one side and a different color on the other side and there were four tiles in the current stack. I had to remember all of the colors in the stack of four and tell my partner what the color of the bottom tile was. My partner would then give me a new tile with a new color. I would then make that the top tile in the stack and repeat. It is SUPER easy, except that it is so easy that the mind gets bored.

I did this with a couple of different people and the results were always similar. Any given person would cap out at a certain range. 5-8 iterations, 21-32 iterations, 45-53 iterations, etc. With practice they moved to a new range. Pretty much everyone who got past 100 could then go on to do it forever. It was an exercise of training the mind to stay on task.

Hope this helps

comments.title: Blessing and Metta
comments.content:
Hi JMG

In one of your replies in the thread about malefic magic above, you mention a monthly practice of blessing all beings to lift one to a level beyond the effects of noxious energies. This brought to mind something I've been meaning to ask about for some time. In the backstory of the Metta Sutta (teaching on Loving Kindness) we hear about how a group of monks the Buddha had sent into a forrest to meditate get molested by evil spirits. They return to the Buddha terrified and asking for help. He then teaches them how to send blessings like "may all beings be well" out in all directions and sends them back into the forrest. It seems to have done the trick, the nasty beings were pacified and the monks found their serenity. Tradition has it that Metta practice can ward off all manner of unpleasantness and calamity.

1) What do you think about this type of pratice as a daily protective practice for those who do not do ritual banishings? Could one use the candle as part of a daily practice?

2) What's going on here from the occult perspective?

3) The old AODA site used to have links to a number of sister orders, among them, I seem to recall, something like the Modern Essenes, which had teachings about this type of blessing practice. I would be interested if these teachings became available again, but in the meantime could you give some pointers for further study?

Many thanks and heartfelt blessings for you and your work.

Mike

replies.title: Re: Blessing and Metta
replies.content:
Interesting.

1) I'd say give it a try. Theoretically speaking, at least, it should work well.

2) It's a matter of the level of the astral plane to which your mind is attuned. The kind of impersonal compassionate benevolence involved in this kind of practice resonates well up on the upper astral. As you attune to that level, that's the "vibe" you send out into the world, and since what you put into the world shapes what you get back from the world, you tend to get more positive results.

3) Yes, the Modern Essene Fellowship was one of the sister orders of AODA. It's currently dormant, but worth reviving. The first practice taught to apprentices is the Blessing Walk; you simply make it a point to take a walk each day, location up to you, and every time you encounter a person or any other living thing, silently direct a blessing toward them, with words such as "May you be blessed." Don't do anything to call attention to yourself; just silently will that every living thing you meet be blessed. It's worth doing; you might consider giving it a try.

replies.title: Re: Blessing and Metta
replies.content: Can you elaborate on why this kind of general, all-purpose blessing is on the "approved" list of things you can do without explicit permission? Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Blessing and Metta
replies.content:
First of all, the Essene work is mystical, not magical. Different paths have different rules.

Second, it's totally nonspecific. You're not trying to change anything at all about the people and beings you're blessing; you're not trying to influence them; you're simply directing at them a wish that they be blessed. Thus it doesn't fall afoul of the rule against trying to influence someone's life without their permission.

replies.title: Re: Blessing and Metta

comments.title: The History of the AWEN
comments.content:
Dear John Michael,

It came up in a druidry forum this morning, so I figured I'd ask an expert. What's the brief history of the AWEN, both as a visual symbol of the three rays of light, and as a sound/tone for magic? Is it older than OBOD as a visual emblem? When does it first appear in written form?

replies.title: Re: The History of the AWEN
replies.content:
The three rays are a medieval symbol -- you'll find them in Bernard Sylvester's Cosmographia, one of the great works of 12th century Platonist mysticism -- and are all through Iolo Morganwg's Barddas, which he began to compile in the late 18th century.

The word is also medieval -- Giraldus Cambrensis mentions Welsh mediums called "awenyddion," "those with awen," in his 13th century Description of Wales. Until quite recently in Druid circles, it was whispered rather than being chanted; in many of the old Druid orders, it was the Grand Druid Word, communicated secretly the way that Masons communicate the Mason Word.

comments.title: Geomantic chart

Hi JMG,

I have recently been contemplating crafting a mojo for career success based on the hoodoo system outlined by Cat Yronwode (with some additional advice from Jason Miller’s work). I decided to perform a divination to see what the outcome would be, and got the following:

1: Fortuna Minor
2: Puella
3: Via
4: Carcer
5: Via
6: Amissio
7: Conjunctio
8: Caput Draconis
9: Caput Draconis
10: Conjunctio
11: Acquisition
12: Tristitia

Presuming the relevant houses here are the 8th (magic) and 10th (career), it looks like this operation is unlikely to be successful, as the only mode of perfection is Caput Draconis in the 8th moving to the 9th making a trine sinister with my own signifier. Meanwhile the 10th house shows the end of the matter as restriction and separation. What’s interesting is that Caput Draconis is a beneficial sign, and the conjunction with Conjunctio in the 10th house is also a good indicator; the problem is that my significator, Fortuna Minor, does not move into perfection with this cluster of helpful signs. This seems to imply that the failure predicted here lies with me, not with the magic; that the mojo would indeed generate career opportunities for me, but I would ultimately fail to take advantage of them, probably due to counting too much on outside help (Fortuna Minor) rather than taking constructive action myself.

My questions, then, are:

1:Would you say this is an accurate reading of the chart?

2:If so, is it possible to achieve success in spite of a reading like this by changing the negative patterns of behavior that the chart identifies as the the failure mode? In other words, if I take the reading as a warning of how I'm likely to sabotage my own success, might I avoid the predicted failure? Or would it be inadvisable to attempt it when I've received negative omens?

Thanks as always for taking the time to answer our questions!

replies.title: Re: Geomantic chart
replies.content: Success or failure in a house chart is shown by whether your significator in the first has any relationship between the significator of the quesited -- in this case, I'd use the 8th. An aspect, in my experience, is not enough to perfect the chart. So the answer is that the working will not achieve the success you want. I'd read Caput Drac passing into conjunction with Conjunctio as simply an indication of what you're trying to get, not something that's actually available to you -- so I'd say it's inadvisable to make the attempt.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Not so much a question this week, but an interesting observation I recently had regarding past and present lives.

After finishing my big clean and renovation projects, I got it in my head to get into woodworking. I've always had an interest and messed around with my Dad's tools when I was a kid, but as a young girl it wasn't something anyone really encouraged, even if I was a dedicated fan of the New Yankee Workshop on PBS. But I'm kind of at this bottoming out of my life right now, and why not try something new, so I started poking around the internet, buying some basic carving tools, finally starting to make walking sticks out of the small trees I'd cut down last summer and stored in the garage because I couldn't just throw them out.

In a very lovely, effortless way, some really fitting resources flowed my way, financially and educationally. I always call these "green lights" so I've proceeded in planning out buying some tools and building a workbench and seeing what I can do, even though it all kind of seems off-the-wall for a 40-something woman to suddenly get into. It's felt a bit like someone else is driving the car, but I like where it is going, so okay.

In the midst of this, I began to get these very vivid flashes of wood chips on a pine forest floor, and not like any pine forest I've walked in the Northeast. Much bigger trees, and a colder, foggier, more mountainous atmosphere, like what I imagine in the Pacific Northwest, though I've never been there. If I meditate on that flash, I get this sense of being a large, rather burly man living in a woodsy cabin and working - albeit roughly - with wood, sometime around the turn of the century. There's oil and sharp edges and a lot of quietness, like a place off the beaten track. I feel like I could be making this up but it has a more distant feel to it, like I have to put some effort into perceiving anything and then just have to accept what comes, which is like the flashes of other things I've experienced before. I've never had such clarity about a previous life before, though.

I've been skeptical about your theory that gender dysphoria relates to past lives because the feminist in me starts shouting that women have the human right to present themselves and live their lives any way they wish -- which was the sociological stance that has helped me the most. But perceiving a previous life as a man in this way shone a different light on things, because no matter how many times I wear boots and flannels I am still not as big, strong and burly as I sometimes perceive myself to be, which has always been at the root of my discomfort. Being able to get my head around the idea of one time actually inhabiting that body has actually been weirdly comforting to me in a "that was then, this is now" kind of acceptance. It's very hard to describe and I doubt I could begin to talk about it anywhere but here, but it's like having such a sense of having once been that particular human allows me to now proceed more fully and comfortably in being this particular human.

Still wearing the boots and flannels, though. ;)

In this life I spent most of my education studying and practicing fine art, Classical Realism, in the hopes of continuing the traditions of the Old Masters. I couldn't do it, because my dyslexia-wired brain not only ruins my handwriting but also my ability to draw freely and beautifully. When I figured this out it kind of crushed me, and I've been more or less at a loss for the past three years about what to do with myself. I do, however, retain a wealth of information about proportion, design, light and shadow, etc. and what I could always do was render value with pencil or charcoal until the cows came home, which I was hoping to translate into the tedious material-shaping of wood carving.

So I'm left wondering now if there's a thread of continuity even in that kind of thing that runs through our incarnations -- that maybe this man I once was worked with wood and trees and tools and that's why I was drawn to it all as a child and now again as an adult searching for purpose. But now I have a background in fine arts that could further elevate the craft, if I can pick it up and (re?)learn the skills. It's both tempting and filled with trepidation at the same time, but honestly right now I don't have anything better to do.

Coincidentally, something about it all, probably in part the relationship with the forest and the trees, brought my interest back around to Druidry, and I now have the freshly-delivered Druidry Handbook and Druid Magic Handbook at my elbow. I hope by now I've at least bought you lunch in book royalties.

Thanks as always for Magic Monday,
Diane

replies.content:

I'd agree, by the way, that women have the right to live their lives however they wish...but that doesn't change the fact that some things happen to human beings (including female ones), rather than being freely chosen by them. You didn't choose your dyslexia, any more than I chose my Aspergers syndrome; nor did the people I know with gender dysphoria choose their dysphoria -- it happened to them, and they had to deal with it as best they could. comments.title: Linear narratives
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

You've spent a lot of time in your blogs critiquing the dominant Faustian narrative of our times, of humanity's progression from the caves to the stars. I appreciate the insights I've gained from your writing, providing alternatives to what I consider an unhealthy value system, a rejection of the natural world as it is and our place within it.

Given this I was surprised to read your thoughts on reincarnation in the Magic Monday FAQ, which seem to me to describe a linear progression from simple organisms, through various animals, to humans (and then beyond?). To me this seems like a rehash of the dominant narrative, that 1) humans are "better" (in some sense) than animals; and that 2) we should strive to transcend the natural world instead of embracing it. Am I misunderstanding reincarnation? I'd love to hear more on this.

Thanks for your time.

-B

replies.title: Re: Linear narratives
replies.content: You're misunderstanding the Druid version of reincarnation, certainly. Humans aren't better than other animals, simply capable of a small but crucial increment in mental complexity -- the increment that allows for reflective thinking, and thus the transition to a different mode of being, which the old Druid teachings call Gwynfydd, "the luminous life." Gwynfydd is not separate from nature; it allows a more complete and conscious engagement with nature than the realm of Abred, or physically incarnate life, can manage. The reason human beings are so clumsy and awkward, as compared to other animals, is that we're stuck in a transitional phase, living two lives at once -- rather like the phase change between liquid water and vapor, neither of which is better than the other...

replies.title: Re: Linear narratives
replies.content: If neither of these modes of being is better than the other, and Gwynfydd offers a more complete engagement with nature, then what does Abred offer differently or more completely than Gwynfydd?

replies.title: Re: Linear narratives
replies.content:
Thanks for this. Somehow I missed your post "A Few Notes on Reincarnation" so I'm going to read through that before I ask any more questions.

All the best,

-B

comments.title: Tsathoggua and Kemet
comments.content:
Good evening JMG,

I just got the notice that my wonderful little St. Toad statue is on its way! I am surprised to find I have rather strong feelings about it, and that the massive elm tree in our yard has already told me that it wants the statue to live out there, in a hidden nook at its base. I'd just thought that He would be a lovely addition to our bookshelves, where I could look at and appreciate the statue every now and again. I have mixed feelings about it going into the yard instead. This is a bit more than I expected.

Anyways, my actual question- have you, or anyone here, had much experience with the Kemetic Orthodox Church? I've gotten the general vibe from their websites and the public portions of their online forums. I appreciate their emphasis on action and volunteering out in the world to multiply the good. Curious if you or anyone here has any direct experience that they would care to comment on.

Many thanks,
Bonnie

replies.title: Re: Tsathoggua and Kemet
replies.content: Fascinating. No, I don't know much about the whole Kemetic scene -- my work with the Neteru has taken place entirely in a Golden Dawn context, which some Egyptian Reconstructionists find offensive, so I've generally stayed well away. I had enough trouble with Celtic Reconstructionist bullies...

replies.title: Re: Tsathoggua and Kemet
replies.content:
FWIW, these folks seem to be pretty non judgemental about what others do (at least on the public forums). They are particular about what they do, but are very clear about "this is what we do for -reasons- and we're serious about that, but how the Netjeru relate to others outside of the KOC is their business."

It's one of the things I found refreshingly mature about them.
